description: An interactive drag-and-drop mini-lab where students test six different tumbler materials (glass, steel, plastic, clay, cloth bag, paper cone) to discover which ones can hold water. Built around the Class 6 NCERT chapter 'Materials Around Us', it teaches material properties — hardness, absorbency, transparency, and waterproofness — through hands-on experimentation with animated water-pouring feedback.
subject: Science
ncertReference: Curiosity — Textbook of Science, Class 6, Chapter 6: Materials Around Us
learningObjectives: Identify materials that can hold water (waterproof, non-absorbent)
Identify materials that cannot hold water (absorbent, soft)
Connect a material's properties to its suitability for a specific use
Practice the scientific method: predict, test, observe, conclude

width.description: Maximum width of the tool container in pixels (component is fully responsive)

height.description: Container height. 'auto' lets content flow naturally

showModeSelector.description: Show or hide the mode-tab pills in the header

enabledModes.description: Which mode tabs to render. Use a subset to focus the activity

animationSpeed.description: Animation speed multiplier. 1 = normal, 2 = 2x faster, 0.5 = half speed

autoPlayDuration.description: Auto-advance delay in ms. 0 disables auto-play

themeColor.description: Primary brand color (indigo from design system)

additionalProps.description: Tool-specific dynamic content. Pass any of these to customise the lab without code changes.

materials.description: Override the default six tumblers with a custom list. Each entry defines one tumbler tile.

id.description: Unique slug, e.g. 'glass', 'steel'. Used as React key.

name.description: Display name shown on the tile, e.g. 'Glass'

emoji.description: Single emoji icon representing the material visually

color.description: Background color of the tile (hex). Use a soft tint.

accentColor.description: Border and label color (hex). Should contrast with `color`.

holdsWater.description: true → tumbler glows green and water fills it. false → tumbler shakes red and water leaks.

reason.description: One-line explanation shown when locked. Keep < 80 chars.

instructionText.default: Drag each tumbler under the water jug. Note which materials hold water and which do not.
instructionText.description: Instruction banner text shown to the student at the top

successMessage.description: Message shown after every material has been tested

showProperties.description: Toggle the 'Latest finding' card with the property label

autoReset.description: Auto-reset the lab after celebration completes

primaryColor.description: Indigo accent — gradients, focus rings, banners

secondaryColor.description: Orange accent — labels, badges, secondary highlights

default_lab.description: Default — six tumblers from the NCERT chapter

kitchen_only.description: Narrow the activity to four kitchen tumblers

materials.name: Glass
materials.reason: Hard, smooth, non-absorbent — water stays in.

materials.name: Steel
materials.reason: Hard metal with no gaps — water cannot escape.

materials.name: Cloth
materials.reason: Cloth absorbs water — it leaks through fibres.

materials.name: Paper
materials.reason: Paper softens when wet and tears apart.

value.instructionText: Drag each kitchen item under the jug. Which would you pick to carry water home?

absorbency_focus.description: Focus on absorbent vs non-absorbent — emphasises one property

materials.name: Sponge
materials.reason: Sponge soaks up water like a thirsty material.

materials.name: Towel
materials.reason: Cotton fibres trap water inside themselves.

materials.name: Plastic Cup
materials.reason: Plastic is waterproof — water has nowhere to go.

materials.name: Metal Can
materials.reason: Sealed metal walls block water completely.

value.instructionText: Test which materials soak up water and which keep it out!

ancient_pottery.description: Tied to the NCERT 'Do you know?' box on Sindhu-Sarasvatī pottery

materials.name: Terracotta Pot
materials.reason: Baked clay forms a hard, sealed pot — water stays inside.

materials.name: Wet Clay Lump
materials.reason: Unbaked clay dissolves and crumbles in water.

materials.name: Leaf Cup
materials.reason: A folded fresh leaf can briefly hold water.

materials.name: Bark Bowl
materials.reason: Bark is porous — water seeps through quickly.

value.instructionText: Imagine you live 4,000 years ago. Which container would you trust to fetch water?
value.successMessage: You discovered why our ancestors invented terracotta!

dark_theme.description: Dark mode for low-light or evening lessons

quick_quiz.description: Hide modes and show only the lab — for in-class quick quiz

value.instructionText: Quick quiz! Test all six and beat the timer.

learn.label: Learn
learn.description: Read about waterproof vs absorbent materials with worked examples

practice.label: Practice
practice.description: Predict-then-test challenge — student guesses before dragging

real_world.label: Real World
real_world.description: Connects findings to everyday objects (kitchen, market, transport)

hands_on.label: Hands-On
hands_on.description: Pure drag-and-drop sandbox — the default experience

steps.description: One step per material tested. Step counter advances as the student drops each tumbler into the test zone.

behaviors.mobile: Single-column layout, smaller font sizes, touch-drag enabled, 3-column tumbler tray
behaviors.tablet: Single-column layout, medium fonts, both touch and mouse input
behaviors.desktop: Two-column split (lab left, results right), full animations

responsive.touch: Custom touch handlers replicate native HTML5 drag-and-drop on mobile

accessibility.keyboard: All tumblers are clickable as a fallback to dragging
accessibility.ariaLabels: Water jug, drop zones, and result chips have aria-label attributes
accessibility.reducedMotion: Animations respect short durations (< 2s); future enhancement: prefers-reduced-motion media query

designSystem.spacing: 24px standard padding, 4px gap inside button groups
